text.title: Tokio Async Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Common Rust/Tokio mistakes: MutexGuard across await, blocking in async, unstructured spawn, nested runtimes.

antipattern.description: Calling blocking syscalls (std::fs::read, reqwest::blocking, image processing) directly from an async function.
antipattern.reason: Blocks the Tokio worker thread for the duration of the syscall. On a 4-thread runtime, 4 simultaneous blocking calls starve all other async tasks until they complete.

antipattern.description: Calling `tokio::runtime::Handle::block_on` or `Runtime::block_on` from within an async context.
antipattern.reason: Panics with "cannot block the current thread from within a runtime" — a cryptic error that appears in tests. Test functions must use `#[tokio::test]` or `block_on` in a sync context only.

antipattern.description: Spawning every task with `tokio::spawn` without awaiting or aborting the returned `JoinHandle`.
antipattern.reason: Orphaned handles keep tasks alive indefinitely. Task panics and errors are silently discarded. Prefer `try_join!` or `JoinSet` for structured concurrency; use `spawn` only for true fire-and-forget background tasks.

antipattern.description: Using `std::sync::Mutex` and holding the guard across an `.await` point.
antipattern.reason: The Mutex guard is not `Send`. The compiler rejects this with a wall of trait-bound errors, but the intent is to block the async thread — which must use `tokio::sync::Mutex` or restructure to not hold the lock across yield points.

antipattern.description: Placing a non-cancel-safe future (DB transaction, multi-step file write) on a `tokio::select!` branch.
antipattern.reason: When a `select!` branch is not selected, the future is dropped at the next `.await` point, leaving partial state. Only cancel-safe primitives (recv, sleep, AsyncRead) belong on select branches.
